data.title: ServiceNow: A Defensive Tech Play With AI-Driven Growth Revival
data.description:
With a market likely to pressure valuations where narratives are not supportive, ServiceNow, Inc''s (NYSE:NOW) maturing profile becomes a strong reason for its positioning as defensive tech now. The last one year has been roughly flat and now with valuation pressures, as well as a psychological resistance of $1000, we could be looking at a Buy thesis that crucially hinges on the entry point at this moment.

The fundamentals, as we will see in this analysis, are still strong. Valuations justify the fundamentals despite looking expensive - although growth assumptions need to be monitored for execution and tech risks. All in all, at around $900, I would recommend a starter position in ServiceNow - a Buy with caution candidate with moderate return expectations.

Revenue Growth and Quality

Despite the scale of above $3 billion in quarterly revenues, ServiceNow's growth at scale remains impressive. In fact, Q2 2025 saw a rebound in growth with YoY growth improving to ~22% after slipping below 20% in Q1. The rebound in Q2 is largely attributable to ServiceNow's AI integrated platform focus that drove over 50% uptick in AI related deals.

This surge is a very strong validation of ServiceNow's AI strategy and innovation roadmap. Upgrading workflows to enable predictive analytics, automated recommendations and virtual agents is now no longer a moat, but a requirement for enterprise workflow solutions to remain relevant. ServiceNow's AI roadmap looks clear and tangible. The acquisition of AI startup Moveworks, for example, is expected to enhance ServiceNow's AI capabilities, particularly in customer service management. There are similar partnerships and smaller acquisitions that ServiceNow actively pursues to keep its AI roadmap updated. A broadening AI enhancement spilling over from IT services into customer service, HR, and security appears like enough AI related upgrades in the tank to allow for decent reinvigorated growth levels, like seen in Q2.

ServiceNow's strengths in terms of a recurring revenue friendly subscription model are already well appreciated and absorbed into valuations. Almost 97% of the revenues are a high quality recurring revenue, and that has been the case for quite a few quarters now, indicating ServiceNow's maturity as a SaaS model.

Future revenue visibility is also extremely strong with Q2 remaining performance obligations of $23.9b representing a 29% YoY growth. Revenue quality indicators also look excellent. The number of customers with an ACV of over $20m grew 30% YoY. Renewal rates are around 98% and mitigate the "larger than average" customer cohort renewal risk in Q4 2025.

Overall, revenue growth has revived and the AI tailwinds are clearly leading those gains, inspiring confidence in further AI led upsells and near term monetization. Other aspects of customer quality from retention to revenue visibility is a continuation of the bullish thesis that has supported ServiceNow for a while now.

Margin Outlook

The free cash flow margin sits at around the early thirties, pushing the combined margin-revenue growth metric sum over 50, almost elite SaaS territory and very impressive for a maturing SaaS player. The impressive cash conversion opens up reinvestments into further AI enhancements and R&D. Eventually, the AI wave could open the next leg of gross margin uptick (by reducing service delivery and support costs over time), which is otherwise quite healthy already, hovering around the 80% mark.

While gross margins have broadly saturated over the past few years, operating margin has improved with scale, with the latest improvements from 2022-23 pushing operating margins from low single digits to the teens now. ServiceNow is scaling efficiently, making the case for a tech play that has become both reasonably profitable and capital efficient. That with a 20%+ growth profile makes ServiceNow's case that of what makes for a strong defensive bet.

I expect the operating margins to continue to grow up to ~15% over the next 1-2 years. Gross margins may start to move over 80% too, after a few more quarters of AI monetization. Even if FCF conversion stagnates where it is today, ServiceNow's bull thesis (fundamentally speaking) still looks strong.

Valuation - High but Fair

In terms of valuations, ServiceNow's EV to forward revenue ratio has now contracted to ~14x, down from ~20x in 2024 (also slightly below the 5 year average of ~15.2x). However, in comparison with other enterprise SaaS leaders like Workday, Inc. (WDAY) and Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), the premium is now higher than in the recent past. This is why I think that despite the valuation differential with peers, and that in an environment where I myself have argued that valuation premiums will be challenged, ServiceNow is valued fairly.

  • ServiceNow is growing at a different scale compared to peers. Salesforce clocked a sub 10% growth in Q2. Workday grew by ~12% in Q2.
  • Salesforce and Workday do not show superior cash flow conversion. In fact the revenue growth-margin number is elite for ServiceNow, even in comparison with peers who barely cross the 40 mark.

  • ServiceNow's offerings address a broader TAM compared to Salesforce's CRM focus and Workday's HCM/finance focus.
  • A tangible and early validation of its AI strategy keeping its competitive position intact
  • There is scope for operating margin gains compared to more mature defensive enterprise SaaS companies like Salesforce.
  • My broad market outlook is not in contradiction to my ServiceNow Buy thesis. I believe valuations will be challenged wherever it is narrative driven and has less supportive evidences to show. ServiceNow has measurable and tangible narratives.

Risks, Outlook and Still a Buy

The risks to ServiceNow's Buy thesis are not beyond the average tech risks like an assumed tech relevance that keeps its competition at bay. Investors will need to keep an eye on not only ServiceNow's progress, but inroads made by competition that now also includes Microsoft in addition to other strong enterprise SaaS players like Salesforce and Workday.

The assumed growth rate in the high teens over the next several years also elevate the execution risks and requires ServiceNow to be on top of its competition for a sustained period of time. That is not under threat right now, but requires monitoring. As of now, a downtrend on R&D spends shows plateauing AI spend for every revenue gained. The R&D spend still remains healthy, helped by a strong cash flow conversion that helps drive R&D ramp-ups (like that seen from pre-COVID to around 2023 where ServiceNow heavily invested in AI and cloud initiatives).

ServiceNow has not had a history of significant revenue surprises. I don't expect revenue surprises in Q3 either, with revenue likely to come in somewhere above ~$3.35 billion. Non-GAAP EPS may come in better than the expected $4.27 mark on the back of AI synergies starting to show up in margins. I will keep an eye on the sub-plots over the headline beats though. ServiceNow's Buy thesis hinges on ongoing AI traction and that leading to margin benefits (although the margin benefits have a couple of quarters more to start showing up meaningfully, in my opinion).

Overall, I recommend a cautious Buy before the earnings results and expect better than expected EPS and margins to support short term upsides. Long term investors can expect single digit returns from this defensive stock, entering at current levels. The thesis only breaks if ServiceNow clocks growth below 18% consistently - indirectly pointing towards other systemic faults like AI monetization and margin stagnation. But as of now, all looks good for long term patient capital.

data.title: High Yield And Tax Advantaged Income Funds From NEOS
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In recent months, NEOS Investments has launched some high yield, tax efficient exchange-traded funds ("ETFs") which are unique and compelling. In essence, NEOS takes index funds and cash then uses options to generate income on the volatility of the fund. As expected, the more volatile an index fund, the more it generates in income. What’s unique about NEOS is their use of section 1256 option contracts which have the distinct advantage of having distributions taxed at 60% long term and 40% short term capital gains, vs. the usual ordinary income rates on most option contracts. Years ago as I was trading options, I discovered these 1256 contracts, however the size of the contracts in amounts such as $1.8 million were too large for an individual to trade.

NEOS offers 13 ETFs which can be broken into three segments: high income, hedged equity income, and enhanced income. In this article I focus on the seven high income funds broken into equity (4) and alternatives (3).

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the ETFs are:

  • Monthly distributions with high yields generating substantial income payments.
  • Tax efficiency using the 1256 Index option contracts being taxed at 60% long term.
  • Upside of capital appreciation from the index fund.
  • Diversification using multiple funds focused on different sectors.
  • Professional management with experience to manage the options trades (vs. do it yourself).
  • Public listings with liquidity, easy to trade, given size and volume of trading.

NEOS Investments

Based in Westport, Connecticut, the company was founded by Troy Cates and Garrett Paolella. Both have decades of experience in trading options and managing ETFs. The name NEOS stands for “Next Evolution Options Strategies.” According to their website:

NEOS ETFs aim to deliver the next evolution of options strategies, where seeking income is the outcome. Built on decades of research and experience, NEOS ETFs aim to empower the investor with portfolio building blocks to provide high income, tax efficiency, and diversification through data-driven options-based ETFs.

The benefits of options-based ETFs include high monthly income generated from writing options, a lower correlation to traditional investment such as stocks and bonds, and finally tax efficiency using index options classified as Section 1256 contracts, which are taxed at 60% long term, 40% short term capital gains vs. ordinary income.

Most of the distributions are treated as return of capital (ROC) which lowers the cost basis of the investment. Transactions are listed in Form 1099 statements which will be sent out by brokerage firms. This return of capital can be tax efficient as the investor won’t be taxed until they sell the investment.

NEOS High Income ETFs

The table below shows the relevant metrics for each fund, separated into equity focused ETFs first, then Alternative investments.

EQUITY HIGH INCOME

The following four funds utilize Index options classified as section 1256 contracts, which are more tax efficient using the 60/40 tax rates.

NEOS S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (SPYI) is similar to investing in the equity make up of the S&P 500 Index (SP500). Income is generated by a combination of call option strategies (buying and selling) using SPX Index options (SPX) and tax loss harvesting. As a secondary goal, the fund may generate capital appreciation in rising markets but will most likely lag the actual S&P500. The majority (96%) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI) is more volatile than SPYI given its heavier weighting to technology stocks, modeling the equity makeup of the Nasdaq-100 Index (QQQ). Income is generated by a combination of call option strategies (buying and selling) using NDX Index options and tax loss harvesting. As a secondary goal, the fund may generate capital appreciation in rising markets but will most likely lag the actual Nadaq-100. The majority (99% recently) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (IWMI) is focused on small cap stocks using the Vanguard Russell 2000 ETF (VTWO). Income is generated by a combination of call option strategies (buying and selling) using the Russell 2000 (RTY) Index options and tax loss harvesting. As a secondary goal, the fund may generate capital appreciation in rising markets. The majority (85% recently) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

NEOS ETF Trust - NEOS MSCI EAFE High Income ETF (NIHI) invests in global stocks ex-U.S., using the iShares Core MSCI EAFE ETF (IEFA). Income is generated by a combination of call option strategies (buying and selling) using Index options and tax loss harvesting. As a secondary goal, the fund may generate capital appreciation. The majority (100% recently) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

HIGH INCOME ALTERNATIVES

The next three funds do not use the Section 1256 Index contracts.

NEOS Bitcoin High Income ETF (BTCI) takes advantage of the high volatility in Bitcoin (BTC-USD) using options to generate income. The fund invests in Bitcoin ETPs for capital appreciation and writes calls on Bitcoin Futures ETFs. Specifically it invests in Vaneck Bitcoin ETF (HODL) which accounts for 22.83% of the portfolio, and iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) at 1.37%. The majority (95% recently) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

Full disclosure, this is the first fund I’ve ever invested in that holds Bitcoin or any type of cryptocurrency. The way I saw it, this fund generates income primarily from the volatility of Bitcoin, which makes sense in my view. Therefore, I see it as an options trading vehicle more than a speculative bet on a relatively new asset class. The actual Bitcoin holdings only account for 24.2% of the portfolio. This means that if Bitcoin fell in value, the fund could still make money off the volatility.

NEOS Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI) invests in U.S. listed real estate investment trusts that track the Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index. For example, the top 3 holdings are Welltower Inc (WELL), Prologis (PLD), and American Tower Corp. (AMT). Income is generated by call option strategies. As a secondary goal, the fund may generate capital appreciation. The majority (79% recently) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

This fund is one of the newer ETFs launched in January 2025. While I’m heavily invested in REITs, the 11.1% distribution yield is higher than any other fund I own. Therefore, I will consider switching some of the laggards to IYRI.

NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI) is attractive to investors that would like to hold gold but are concerned that it doesn’t generate income and may fall in value. The fund invests in physical gold for capital appreciation and generates income on options strategies using Gold ETPs. Specifically the fund holds 24.34% of the portfolio in Goldman Sachs Physical Gold ETF (AAAU). The majority (90% recently) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

Performance

An investment of $10,000 in SPYI from inception in 2022 would have yielded a total return of $16,680 today. In comparison, an investment in the S&P500 - SPY would have yielded $19,250, showing an investor gives up some return as call options are exercised.

Similarly, an investment of $10,000 in QQQI over the past approximate two years, yields $13,790 vs. the NASDAQ index - QQQ at $14,240.

Risks

The stock market is currently trading at record level highs with many highly visible stocks trading at rich valuations. The potential for a correction is high, in my view. Although the market is shrugging off concerns about a government shutdown, it could prove to be a catalyst for a sell-off if not settled in a timely manner.

Geopolitical risks are still high around the world as the war continues in Ukraine as Trump was unable to broker a deal with Russia, and Russian war planes have flown over NATO countries. The risks include military action which could disrupt oil prices and supply chains.

Within each ETF the option portion caps some of the upside on each fund, so an investor needs to understand that long term, the total return would be higher if one just bought and held the index.

Expectations of a second and third rate cut in 2025 are high. As both inflation and unemployment are rising, the Fed may be pressured to hold rates if inflation rose too high as a result of tariff policy. That change in rate projections would alter the valuations of stocks that stood to benefit from a lower cost of borrowing.

Bottom Line

I recommend NEOS funds to income focused investors seeking high monthly distributions with potential for capital appreciation. The NEOS portfolio of high income ETFs offer significant benefits in the form of income generation from option strategies, potential tax efficiency, lower volatility, and total returns.

The funds are especially interesting because the distribution rates are high, in my view. For example, QQQI is currently yielding 14% vs. JPMorgan’s (JEPQ) at 10.46%. NEOS generates income from trading options on the underlying security. These index options used on the Equity High Income ETFs are classified as section 1256 contracts, which are subject to lower taxes at 60% long term and 40% short term capital gains. Overall, the goal is to use their ETFs for equity allocations, such as the S&P 500, Nasdaq, real estate etc. realizing there will be higher income but lower capital appreciation making up total return.

In the comment section below, I would be interested to hear you thoughts about these funds and potential fit within your portfolio.

data.title: 60/40 Portfolio Comeback: AOR ETF Matches S&P 500 Return So Far (Downgrade)
data.description:
The 60/40 portfolio is having a great year. On a total return basis, the iShares Core 60/40 Balanced Allocation ETF (AOR) has returned 14%. That's an identical performance compared to the S&P 500 SPDR ETF (SPY).

Of course, AOR’s material weighting in international stocks, along with healthy returns in both U.S. and ex-U.S. bonds, has helped comparisons to previous years in which the market’s story was all about U.S. large-cap stocks.

I had a "Buy" rating on AOR back in March 2023, around the time of the regional banking crisis. Shares have returned 43% in that span. While that’s less than the S&P 500’s 73% advance, from a risk perspective, the Sharpe Ratio has been solid. But I am downgrading AOR to a "Hold" today.

Global yields are lower, while equity valuations have ticked up.

AOR & SPY: Same Return in 2025

According to the issuer, the iShares Core Growth Allocation ETF (AOR) is meant to help investors "build a diversified core portfolio focused on growth using one low-cost fund." AOR uses iShares ETFs to construct a portfolio that tracks a 60/40 stock/bond asset allocation strategy with a focus on growth and a target risk level.

AOR has grown substantially since my previous analysis, with total assets under management of $2.8 billion, up from $2.0 billion in March 2023. Its annual expense ratio remains low at only 15 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is more than a percentage point above that of the S&P 500 at 2.48%.

Share-price momentum has been solid ever since the April low, earning AOR a decent B- ETF Grade by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. I’ll note later in the article, however, that the RSI momentum has weakened. In terms of risk, the fund has rather low volatility, thanks to its ample global and asset-class diversification.

As far as liquidity goes, AOR is highly tradeable. Its average daily volume is north of 200,000 shares, while the 30-day median bid/ask spread is tight at just two basis points, per iShares.

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star, Silver-rated ETF by Morningstar’s equity exposure plots along the top row of the style box, indicating the high weight to global large caps. What’s key to realize with AOR is that it is a fund of funds, meaning the holdings are not individual equities, but a basket of ETFs. Today, the price-to-earnings ratio is somewhat elevated above 19x on a real-time basis.

Michael Hartnett at Bank of America points out that U.S. and foreign stocks are richly priced and certainly not as attractive compared to where they traded back in March 2023. Moreover, global bonds now yield about 4%, down from 5% a couple of years ago.

AOR: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Global Stocks: Multi-Year High in the P/E

As far as the sector composition, Information Technology commands the largest weight at more than 27%, while Financials (which has struggled lately) is the next biggest at 16%. Industrials and Consumer Discretionary (two cyclical sectors) are the only other two areas with a more than 10% weight.

AOR: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, October has been about flat in terms of its average price return going back 10 years, according to Seeking Alpha’s data. November has been by far the best of the 12 months, historically speaking. So, perhaps we see a retreat before a rally later in the quarter.

AOR: Bullish November Trends

The Technical Take

With a higher equity valuation today and smaller yields in the fixed income “40” portion, AOR’s technical situation is generally healthy. There are some caution signals, however. First, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart (hinted at earlier) printed a bearish negative divergence to price at the recent high. That should at the very least suggest a dip toward the rising short-term 50-day moving average.

Also take a look at the volume by price profile. There are not many shares traded until AOR dips to about $59. That’s where I see support. It would likely require a full-blown global stock market correction (down 10%) to see AOR test that level. What’s more, the long-term 200dma comes into the picture just above $60. Finally, a measured move upside price objective to $67 is in play based on the $53 to $60 range that was broken through in the second quarter.

AOR: Weaker RSI Momentum, Support Near $60

The Bottom Line

I have a "Hold" rating on AOR. Global equity and bond valuations are richer today compared to a quarter-decade ago. I expect softer gains in the quarters ahead, with a possible near-term dip.

data.title: Curtiss-Wright: My Reasons To Stay Optimistic About Further Upside
data.description:
Curtiss-Wright Corporation (NYSE:CW) is making strides on its commercial nuclear operations in the wake of the U.S. Government’s plans for the next 2 decades. This segment has been sidelined historically amid fears of negative public reaction, but there is a lot of potential going forward. With a strong business pipeline covering its end market segments, the stock has been rallying so far this year, outperforming the broader S&P 500 benchmark index by a stretch.

In this article I will uncover some of the recent developments, not just on the business front, but also in relation to the company’s aggressive share buyback program. The program itself has been a catalyst for such a surge in share price, leading investors to now become more cautious with valuations. However, with management’s intentions of continuing with repurchases and raising dividends, I see further upside in Curtiss-Wright stock. Here I will present my bullish stance.

Segments

Curtiss-Wright operations are trifurcated into three segments, i.e., Aerospace & Industrial, Defense Electronics, and Naval & Power. However, for analysis, it is important to look at a more specific classification in terms of two end markets, as presented below.

Aerospace & Defense End Market

  • Aerospace, Ground & Naval Defense

  • Commercial Aerospace

Commercial End Market

  • Power and Process

  • General Industries

From the graph presented above, we can clearly see that the product mix is tilted more towards the A&D market segment, which is expected to contribute 69% to the topline in 2025. Further breakdown of the A&D segment shows that naval defense is forecasted to contribute 26%, followed by 19% from aerospace defense, 13% from commercial aerospace, and 11% from ground defense. However, in terms of a growing backlog of $3.9 billion, both end market segments generated solid demand and exhibited double-digit growth in revenues in the second quarter of 2025. This led to $1 billion worth of new orders during the quarter, showcasing a 15% growth in demand backlog compared to 2024. In the next section, we take a look at the product offerings within each of these end market segments in detail.

Product Mix

Curtiss-Wright supplies many mission-critical technologies to both Aerospace & Defense and Commercial end markets. The product mix is spread across the three main segments of Aerospace and Industrial, Defense Electronics, and Naval and Power.

The company supports its Aerospace and Industrial segment through equipment like actuators, sensors, and process control devices, such as those for monitoring pressure and temperature. These help improve performance and efficiency across all aerospace and industrial applications. The conversion of hydraulic actuators to electromechanical actuators provides an area of growth for the company, as electromechanical systems provide better control and accuracy, very important for modern defense systems.

Moving on to the Defense Electronics segment, this is where the company serves the requirements of electronic warfare, flight safety, unmanned systems, etc. A key area of growth here is the upgrade of avionics systems. For instance, on 16th May 2025, the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) enacted a law passed by the Congress last year, requiring all new aircraft to have at least 25 hours of cockpit voice recording available. Existing aircraft must also meet this requirement by May 2030. This is where the company’s HCR-25 cockpit voice recorder shines, built specifically to cater to this changing regulation. Prior to this, the same product was only utilized for longer durations of recordings, not specifically for 2 hours. The product was already certified for most Boeing aircraft by 2023, but is still awaiting certification for the Airbus A320 series. This is just one example of how the company’s highly reliable electronics systems are adjusting to modern-day requirements across both commercial and defense applications.

The company provides power generation and nuclear propulsion systems through its Naval and Power segment. These are not only essential in U.S. Navy submarines and aircraft carriers but also fit well into the U.S. government’s nuclear programs over the next two and a half decades, which I touch upon later in this analysis. Through these three segments, Curtiss-Wright positions itself as a critical component in the supply chain of U.S. government and commercial operations. In fact, it benefits from leveraging its products through cross-overs across both defense and commercial solutions.

Many of the company’s technologies, like sensors, data acquisition systems, networking and storage equipment, and computing boards, are leveraged across all the segments. In the context of the U.S. government’s Golden Dome plans, such a variety of products allows CW to fit into various subsystems of the larger plan. For instance, its mission control systems, like actuators, ammunition handlers, and aiming and stabilization solutions would fit well into missile launchers and missile interception systems. Similarly, the embedded computing boards built by the Defense Solutions Division are critical in command and control systems, while the data acquisition and recording solutions and networking modules are useful in system monitoring to ensure operational readiness. Curtiss-Wright’s Surface Technologies Division provides the necessary treatments and coatings to extend the lifecycle of mission-critical components. This division helps improve the safety and durability of many of the products the company makes across its business segments. But the same solutions are similarly relevant for a project like the Golden Dome. For national security reasons, it is not always clear where the government is sourcing such necessary equipment from, but Curtiss-Wright, through its multiple divisions, gives itself a great shot at being an integral part of such a national defense system.

Commercial Nuclear Operations – Underrated So Far

Curtiss-Wright’s commercial nuclear operations have largely remained underrated but are now showing clear signs of ambition. Historically, there has been a lot of uncertainty around this segment given a highly negative public narrative against nuclear initiatives worldwide. The narrative stems from a public misconception that nuclear energy is only used for developing nuclear weapons. This confusion completely ignores the immense potential of nuclear energy to reduce carbon emissions. The situation gets exacerbated by anti-nuclear movements that revolve around financial burden on economies and environmental concerns due to the radioactive waste. Such misconceptions have affected companies like Curtiss-Wright, who are a part of the broader supply chain of such operations. However, in recent times this narrative has changed because of a resurgence in low-emission small modular reactors (SMRs) to meet industrial and local energy needs. With the U.S. government planning to quadruple its nuclear capacity in the next 25 years, there are opportunities for industry players like Curtiss-Wright both in the long and short run. On the SMR front, Curtiss-Wright is scaling up through collaborations with several developers like X Energy, LLC (privately held), TerraPower, LLC (also privately held), and Rolls-Royce Holdings plc (OTCPK:RYCEY).

Earlier in January, Curtiss-Wright also completed its $200 million cash acquisition of UK-based Ultra Energy. Ultra Energy is a manufacturer of nuclear reactor control systems, neutron and radiation monitoring systems, and temperature and pressure sensors. These systems have aerospace, industrial, and defense applications, and ensure secure operations of commercial nuclear plants. With such additions to Curtiss-Wright’s product portfolio, demand for commercial nuclear power products remained strong during the first half of 2025. Impact of Ultra Energy on Curtiss-Wright’s power & process end market showed up during the second quarter as the segment revenues totaled $163 million, an 18% growth compared to the same period in 2024. This seems to be in line with the management’s expectations of a fourfold growth in commercial nuclear revenues to $1.5 billion within the next decade.

Apart from maintenance support on existing nuclear plants, Curtiss-Wright can also benefit from greenfield buildouts for zero-carbon SMR plants and The Large Gen III+ nuclear reactors plants. Curtiss-Wright has already cemented its place in this nuclear landscape, as it is a key supplier of coolant pumps for Westinghouse Electric Company’s AP1000 Generation III+ pressurized water reactor plant. With further developments, Curtiss-Wright has a chance to position itself as a key player within the evolving nuclear energy space.

Financials

In the second quarter of 2025, Curtiss-Wright registered a 12% top line growth with figures of $877 million. In terms of end market segments, aerospace & defense revenues were up 12%, and the commercial side of the business grew by 10%. Naval and Power operations continue to lead the overall growth trajectory, with revenues of $384 million during the quarter. With a 19% YoY growth in topline, this part of the business exhibited the highest growth, driven by a strong demand for SMRs and aftermarket support within the commercial nuclear market. The acquisition of Ultra Energy, discussed above, also gave an extra push to this segment. Demand remains robust, with $1 billion worth of new orders following $939 million during the first quarter, resulting in a growth of 12% in the demand backlog so far this year. Operating margins for the second quarter were roughly 18%, with margins from the naval & power segment registering the highest growth of 2.1%. The segment margins stood at 16.5% despite incurring high R&D expenses related to commercial nuclear energy, advanced sensors, and control systems.

The management has revised its 2025 earnings guidance and forecasted overall revenues for the year to exceed $3.4 billion. Estimates for operating income growth have also been revised from the previous 13%-16% range to the 15%-18% range. With the ongoing share repurchase program, full-year EPS is expected to be around 13%, which means a growth in the high-teens. The company anticipates FCF conversion to remain above 105%, reflecting upon Curtiss-Wright’s liquidity and cash conversion efficiency. At the end of the first quarter, 2025 free cash flow guidance was between $495 million and $515 million, which has now been revised to $520 million to $535 million.

Historically the company has delivered decent margins, with a slight uptick in net margins in 2024. Moreover, FCF conversion has been impressive, ranging between 110% - 116% on average. Although there is a slight dip in estimated FCF conversion for 2025, there is nothing concerning on that footing. This puts the company in a strong financial position in relation to its debt servicing capacity. Debt levels have remained around the $1 billion mark, which is roughly 5.6% of CW’s current market capitalization. Despite large sums of share repurchases, the capital structure management remains optimal after the company conducted significant borrowing operations between 2020 and 2023. Curtiss-Wright also continues to extend resources towards cash acquisitions. Having spent more than $1.6 billion on cash acquisitions from 2017 to 2024, the management is still focused on exploring high-margin acquisition opportunities across different business segments. In my view, Curtiss-Wright has the capacity to finance any future acquisitions through internally generated funds rather than relying on debt issues.

Returning Capital – Share Repurchase & Dividends Growth

During the second week of September, Curtiss-Wright expanded its 2025 share buyback program by another $200 million. That would bring the total anticipated share repurchases by the end of 2025 to more than $450 million, the highest ever in a given year. This move comes after successful execution of repurchase programs in previous years, aimed towards value creation and returning capital to shareholders. Since 2020, the company has returned around $900 million through multiple buyback programs as part of its broader capital allocation strategy. As per the management, such buybacks also help in offsetting potential dilution resulting from share-based compensation plans.

The above-mentioned buybacks indicate management’s optimistic view of business operations and free cash flow generation ability. In the revised earnings guidance for 2025, management expects a 105% FCF conversion ratio, which has come down slightly after registering adjusted FCF conversion ratios of 116% in 2024 and 114% in 2023. However, there is nothing to worry about going forward. In fact, the CW board reiterated its confidence in the future outlook by approving a $400 million increase in the repurchase authorization in May, now totaling $534 million. These are the shares that will be at the company’s disposal for executing future buybacks.

During the announcement in May, the board also approved a 14% raise to first quarter dividend payment, from the previous $0.21 to $0.24 a share. This is a notable raise after growing dividends in the range of 5-6% in previous years. In September, the company announced a payment of a $0.24 dividend again for the second quarter, which signals towards sustainability of dividend growth in the future. The forward annualized dividend yield of 0.18% is very low relative to peers and would not excite too many investors. But the ongoing share repurchases and strong capital gains prospects do make up for such low yields.

In my view, the share buybacks will continue in the coming periods as Curtiss-Wright’s existing capital structure does not pose any major risks. With long-term obligations hovering around the $1 billion mark, the company can rely on this strategy to unleash long-term value for shareholders. As stated, an impressive free cash flow conversion further strengthens management’s case for implementing this strategy.

Stock Performance and Peers

Curtiss-Wright has a strong bullish momentum, with the stock trading above the 20, 50, and 200-day moving averages. The Relative Strength is also near 60, suggesting that buyers are in control. The stock has appreciated 72.7% in six months. The fact that the RSI hasn’t gone above 70 since mid-June suggests buyers are in control, but there is no excessive bullish sentiment either. This is a healthy sign and points to the continuation of the uptrend. However, more risk-averse investors would want to buy closer to the 50-day moving average or at a lower RSI so they can avoid getting caught in a sell-off.

Curtiss-Wright is up 56.56% this year so far. It has also comfortably outpaced the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF’s (SPY) 13% YTD returns. More importantly, it has also outpaced the SPDR® S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF’s (XAR) 44% returns. The stock is the sixth largest holding of the ETF at 3.53%.

The outperformance has led to an inflated P/E ratio of 46.44x, slightly above XAR’s P/E ratio of 38.6x. This is by no means an outrageous valuation, and waiting for a correction might result in missing out on future gains while the stock is hot.

Looking at the peers, there are also many companies that offer similar products to Curtiss-Wright, but they do not cover as broadly a portfolio as Curtiss-Wright. I have therefore considered only two companies to compare the valuation to, Moog Inc. (MOG.A) and AMETEK, Inc. (AME), which have an overlap in both the Aerospace and Industrial, and Defense Electronics segments.

Curtiss-Wright is trading at a forward P/E of 42.29x compared to 26.34x for MOG.A and 29.48x for AME. This premium starts making sense when one considers the growth rates of the three companies.

Curtiss-Wright is also expected to grow its earnings at a reasonable growth rate, as shown below. It offers stable earnings owing to its position in the Aerospace supply chain, and that justifies its valuation when other positive factors mentioned in the article are also considered.

Risks

A major risk for Curtiss-Wright lies in its Naval Defense segment. Constituting 26% of the sales, this segment is also anticipated to bring in 7-9% growth by the end of the year.

There is a concern that naval projects are ‘too long term’ and the aircraft carriers and submarines, which take years to build, are at the mercy of low-cost drones and unmanned vessels. One example of a policy shift due to these fears was the DoD Replicator Initiative. The initiative aims to avoid the concentration of combat power into individual expensive platforms like aircraft carriers. A further policy shift in this direction could spell trouble for companies like Curtiss-Wright, which serve the U.S. naval infrastructure.

Curtiss-Wright’s growth prospects in nuclear power also carry a geopolitical and sentiment risk. While the public perception of nuclear energy has started to positively change, some states in the U.S. and some European countries have previously not viewed it as a safe option. This perception can change further due to both geopolitical events and changing governments post-elections. Recent pivots back to nuclear energy in Europe are an example. Despite being a promising growth prospect for Curtiss-Wright, nuclear still carries with it a perception risk.

Last but not least, investors should not ignore the stock price swings risk. I have already pointed out that the stock's valuation is still attractive despite a 51% run YTD. This, however, does not mean the stock is immune from short-term swings. This short term volatility can be used to the investor’s advantage, but it is still a risk, especially if the growth story takes longer to play out.

Conclusion

Given the management’s upward revision of 2025 earnings, I have a very optimistic view on Curtiss-Wright stock for the near-term. Although there are talks of this stock being overbought, resulting in fears of a price correction, I will still place my bets on the 2025 share repurchase program to offer support. Large scale multi-decade projects such as the Golden Dome and nuclear energy expansions offer tailwinds for the long term. My recommendation is a Buy.

data.title: Bradesco Q3 Earnings Preview: Expectation Of Further Improvements
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my buy recommendation on Bradesco shares (NYSE:BBD) in this Q3 earnings preview. This article continues my coverage that started on May 23, 2024, and the last one about the company was published on July 31, 2025. My intention in this article is to present my expectations for the results of the 3rd quarter that Bradesco will release on October 29th.

Corporate Profile

Founded in 1943 in São Paulo, Bradesco has maintained its headquarters in Osasco since 1959. Currently the bank has 74 million customers and 2,163 branches in Brazil. Bradesco does not have a strong international presence; however, it has about 10 own agencies in the US, Europe, and Asia, as well as subsidiaries.

The bank also operates through its digital operation, which has grown rapidly since its implementation in the 1990s. Currently, its main business is credit operations; however, the bank also has insurance, pension, capitalization, and service provision operations.

Revisiting The Thesis

My last article showed the excellent result that Bradesco reported. At the time, I was surprised by the company's resilience, which reported expansion in the credit portfolio combined with control over NPLs.

The most incredible thing is that all of this occurred amid a complex macroeconomic scenario. Now, I will indicate my projections for each segment of the result that the company will report.

I made a point of preparing this article before publishing the results because, in my view, Bradesco should present an attractive and improving valuation dynamic of results. This combination is not seen in other Brazilian banks, since banks with good results trade at high multiples and banks with mediocre results have unattractive multiples.

It is interesting to remember that the bank is Brazilian and publishes its results in local currency, so I will convert considering 1 USD = 5.32 BRL, as this was the rate on the last day of the third quarter.

Credit Portfolio and NPL

For the credit portfolio, I project Bradesco's expanded credit portfolio to reach BRL 1.04 trillion ($195.5 billion) in Q3, up +1.1% q/q and +9.1% y/y. The advance represents a slowdown compared to the pace observed in Q2 (+11.8% y/y), reflecting the bank's strategy of preserving the quality of the portfolio in the face of a still challenging credit environment, marked by high interest rates and a high commitment to family income.

Growth should be driven by the portfolios of SMEs, mainly in government lines with more guarantees and lower risk, and individuals, with greater selectivity in mass credit and greater appetite in the middle- and high-income segments.

My projections for the credit portfolio are based on the results of the last quarters. Even amid a challenging macroeconomic scenario, the company reported growth of +3.5% in Q3 2024, +4.0% q/q in Q4 2024, +2.4% in Q1 2025, and +1.3% in Q2 2025. Therefore, I think it is sensible to project growth of +1.1% q/q in Q3 2025.

For NPLs, I am projecting that NPL > 90d will end the quarter at 4.1%, stable q/q and -40 bps y/y, showing greater selectivity in credit granting, despite the increase in NPLs observed in recent months.

My projections are consistent given that NPL >90d has been stable for two consecutive quarters. Therefore, the projection of stability for another quarter is quite conservative in my view.

NII (Net Interest Income)

I project net interest income of BRL 18.8 billion ($3.53 billion), representing an increase of +3.7% q/q and +16.9% y/y. The quarter's performance should be supported almost entirely by Clients NII, while Market NII should be close to zero.

Additionally, I estimate that NII with clients reaches BRL 18.7 billion ($3.51 billion), with an increase of +5.0% q/q and +19.2% y/y, driven by a more favorable mix and spread improvement.

On the other hand, the market NII should be at just BRL 66 million ($12.4 million) (vs. BRL 289 million ($54.3 million) in Q2 and BRL 365 million ($68.6 million) in Q3 2024), reflecting the higher interest rate environment.

Growth projections for net interest income are also in line with the last four quarters. In this sense, projecting +3.7% in Q3 2025 is consistent with high spreads and moderate portfolio growth.

Allowance For Loan Losses And Cost Of Credit

I estimate that allowance for loan losses will reach BRL 8.6 billion ($1.62 billion), with an increase of +4.7% q/q and +19.3% y/y. The cost of credit should remain relatively stable at 4.5% (+15 bps q/q; +26 bps y/y).

In the last three quarters, the +3.5% in Q3 2024 increased sequentially from BRL 7.1 billion, BRL 7.6 billion, and BRL 8.1 billion. In parallel, the cost of credit was between 3.0% and 3.2%. In this sense, my estimate of BRL 8.6 billion (+4.7% QoQ) represents a new high, natural to the trend.

Expenses

For expenses, I estimate that administrative expenses will be BRL 15.2 billion ($2.86 billion), with an increase of +2.5% q/q and +5.4% y/y, growing in line with inflation and deceleration compared to Q2 2025 (+6.1% y/y). Consolidation of Cielo operations began in August 2024 after the acquirer's OPA. Growth reflects footprint adjustments and higher labor provisions.

After the incorporation of Cielo, I consider it consistent with the history of recent quarters to estimate an increase of 2.5% QoQ and 5.4% YoY in expenses. We must remember that the incorporation distorted the numbers in recent quarters, but they should normalize now.

My Perceptions

In my opinion, the company must report another solid quarter. In addition, I project net income of BRL 24.7 billion ($4.65 billion), representing an increase of +18.5% y/y, mainly due to a higher contribution of the financial result from higher interest rates. Given all the arguments listed above, I consider it feasible to estimate a net profit of $4.65 billion, considering the evolution of results amid the turnaround.

Valuation

I consider that valuation remains attractive with shares trading at 7.6x P/E 2025e and 1.06x P/B, with a dividend yield of 7.6% in the year. In my opinion, a bank the size of Bradesco for the Brazilian economy, and with such a significant improvement in results, should have pricing well beyond 1x P/B.

I will estimate a target of 1.2x P/B, considering that the company has traded well above this level in the past. Therefore, I reiterate my buy recommendation with a target price based on confidence in the gradual improvement of results with the turnaround execution.

Potential Risks To The Thesis

The thesis has several risks. Management has already indicated that the growth of the credit portfolio should come from SMEs and individuals. However, keeping NPLs controlled while being bolder in granting credit to these segments can be challenging. The bank may see an abrupt increase in the cost of credit and have to increase provisions.

Another risk to this thesis is the prospect that the market NII approaches zero. However, the continuity of the complex macroeconomic scenario with interest rates at 15% could dismantle the projections and put pressure on the bank's funding.

Finally, Quant tools from Seeking Alpha recommend that investors hold their shares, and this differs from my recommendation to buy the stock. The thesis is complex, and investors must carefully analyze the case.

The Bottom Line

Despite the challenges, I project another promising result for Bradesco. In my opinion, we will see another quarter with controlled expansion in the credit portfolio and stability in NPLs. This would consolidate the effectiveness of Bradesco's robust turnaround.

At the same time, the stock still trades at just 1.06x P/B. This demonstrates that there is still a good margin of safety to make the investment.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy Bradesco shares in this Q3 earnings preview. Management is being extremely efficient in driving the turnaround, and the attractive risk-return relationship makes this investment quite interesting.

data.title: Pinterest: Try ChatGPT Or Gemini, Then You'll Understand The Valuation
data.description:
Pinterest (NYSE:PINS) carved itself a unique and beautiful niche in the social media landscape, playing the role of a personalized shopping and inspiration platform.

With over 570 million monthly active users, Pinterest certainly isn't the largest platform by any measure, but it's a place that people come to with commercial intent, which is music to advertisers' ears.

Three years into their business transformation, all arrows point in the right direction, and valuation remains undemanding considering the growth trajectory.

I argue that the reason for the cautious valuation is a justified fear of disruption from AI, but still rate the stock a "Buy."

Introduction To Pinterest

In 2009, Ben Silbermann and Paul Sciarra, two of Pinterest's co-founders, launched an app called Tote. The goal of the app was to be a digital replacement for paper catalogs. The app became quite popular, but not for shopping like they intended. Instead, it was used to collect inspiration and ideas to purchase elsewhere, mainly because of the technological limitations at the time.

In 2010, joined by the third co-founder, Evan Sharp, they embraced the idea of being a place people go to for visual discovery and changed the company's name to Pinterest, a combination of the words 'Pin' and 'Interest.'

In the 12 years that followed, Pinterest became the place people go to for ideation and inspiration, but the company failed to capture the value from that intent. In most cases, users found something they liked and then went to Google (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), and others to actually shop. During those years, Pinterest mainly monetized through brand advertising.

Finally, in 2022, after former commerce executive Bill Ready assumed the CEO role, Pinterest sort of went full circle to the Tote days. It transitioned into a personalized shopping platform, where almost everything you see is shoppable. This allowed them to shift the focus to performance marketing, which is a far more lucrative space, especially for digital platforms.

This brings us to today. Even with all the improvements, Pinterest remains a lagging stock. It's up a miserable 34% since its IPO in 2019, down over 60% from its peak, and underperformed the market by a significant gap even during its successful transformation.

Is this an opportunity or a warning sign? Let's find out.

Pinterest's Business Transformation Is Yielding Results

The business transformation started in June 2022, when Bill Ready became CEO. In the roughly three years since, average revenue per user jumped 30%, despite FX headwinds. In the United States & Canada, ARPU surged nearly 50%, and we're witnessing the start of an acceleration story in the rest of the world.

Meanwhile, monthly active users rose from 433 million in Q2 2022 to 578 million last quarter, with growth accelerating to 11% Y/Y in Q2. The fastest-growing regions are the Rest of World, then Europe, then North America.

As a result, revenues reached a Q2 record of $998 million, up 17% Y/Y and roughly 50% from Q2 2022. Here, too, the Rest of World is leading with 65% growth, followed by Europe with 34%, then North America at 11%.

These numbers are not only strong, they are industry-leading. In fact, Pinterest has been a market-share gainer for the past five quarters. Still, though, it's so small, you can't even see it in my graph, so I'll just tell you its market share reached 0.71% in Q2, a June quarter record.

The one area where Pinterest still has a lot of work to do is below the revenue line. They still rely on non-GAAP metrics to show reasonable profits, and I don't like it. Like many others, the primary adjustment here is stock-based compensation, which, in the case of Pinterest, is very meaningful.

For the three months that ended in June, Pinterest had a GAAP operating loss of $4.3 million, a significant improvement from last year, but still a loss. They have a lot of cash and no debt, so they are generating interest income. They have also been losing money for a long time, so they aren't paying taxes. All up, their GAAP net income was $39 million, or $0.06 per share for Q2 2025.

Well, no worries, all you have to do is exclude $227 million in expenses, and there you have it, your net income is nearly 6x higher. Of course, I'm being sarcastic. While they are making improvements and demonstrating operational leverage, there's still a lot more to do before they reach their true profit potential and stop relying on non-GAAP metrics.

The AI Fear

We'll get to valuation later, but there's no need for more than napkin math to conclude that Pinterest is undervalued considering its growth prospects.

When a stock is undervalued, I believe investors must identify the cause before they make an investment decision. If the cause comes down to exaggerated or misguided concerns, then you should buy the stock. If the cause is reasonable and has merit, perhaps you should wait.

When it comes to Pinterest, I believe the main reason for its undervaluation comes down to AI. Specifically, AI assistants like ChatGPT and Gemini are increasingly becoming shopping assistants across many verticals that overlap with Pinterest.

I encourage you to go try it out yourself. You can upload a picture of your living room, tell the chatbots what you want, and they'll give you ideas, including links. They will also let you ask for specific changes. The same is true for clothes, recipes, decoration, and the list goes on.

Critically, one line of defense for Pinterest content used to be the fact that it isn't generated by AI. However, in a recent podcast with the Google Search VP of Product, he specifically says that one area he's very excited about is visual discovery for personal shopping with search, rather than generative AI.

Right now, I still believe Pinterest has a unique offering that can't be replaced by AI. It's also a place where people go for inspiration and don't need to be active in describing what they're looking for. Still, betting against Gemini's and ChatGPT's expansion of capabilities is not a bet I'm happy to take.

Valuation & Outlook

At $33 a share, Pinterest trades at 18 times forward earnings and 15 times next year's estimates, though these are non-GAAP earnings, which I find to be almost irrelevant in this case.

If we assume the ratio of GAAP to non-GAAP earnings is about 1:4, you get a very different picture when it comes to valuation. However, I think the best metric to assess Pinterest at this point is free cash flow.

Over the trailing 12 months, they generated $1 billion in free cash flow, translating to about a 22x P/FCF multiple based on the current market cap.

Meanwhile, Pinterest is a mid-teens growth story, expected to grow revenues at a 15% CAGR over the next three years, alongside consistent margin improvements.

In my view, Pinterest could maintain its multiple while growing FCF at a 20%+ clip, which means expected returns of 20% over the next few years.

Conclusion

I would be much more excited to buy Pinterest at this valuation if it weren't for the AI threat, but the reason we're getting it at this valuation is precisely that.

At 22 times trailing free cash flow, ahead of a multi-year FCF growth trajectory, Pinterest gives investors enough margin of safety in my view.

As such, I rate the stock a "Buy," but I wouldn't make it a huge portion of my portfolio, considering the possibility that Google or OpenAI can launch a killer feature that will harm Pinterest any day.

data.title: Northrim BanCorp: Seeing Past Recent Underperformance
data.description:
One of my absolute best calls in recent years has been Northrim BanCorp, Inc. (NASDAQ:NRIM). Since I first rated it a Buy in August of 2023, the stock has skyrocketed 95.4%. That's almost double the 49.7% jump that the S&P 500 (SP500) has experienced.

Lately, though, things have been more disappointing. In May of this year, I reaffirmed the business as a Buy candidate. During that window of time, the stock has risen only 5%. The market, by comparison, has shot up 18.2%.

This underperformance is disappointing, but I don't believe it's tied to anything related to the company's overall health. The balance sheet continues to expand, the income statement continues to grow, and asset quality remains robust. Shares are expensive on a price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book basis. But relative to earnings, the stock makes for an attractive opportunity.

Hopefully, upcoming earnings figures, which are expected to come out on October 22nd, will continue to paint a rosy picture. At present, analysts expect strong revenue and earnings growth. And with the overall trajectory of the company in recent memory, I would be inclined to agree that some degree of expansion is almost guaranteed. Given this, as well as the other factors I mentioned already, I firmly believe that this still makes for an attractive Buy candidate even though the market has recently disagreed with my assessment.

A great business worth banking on

The newest data that we have regarding Northrim BanCorp covers through the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. No matter how you look at it, the picture here looks positive. Deposits during that quarter came in at $2.81 billion. That's up from the $2.78 billion reported for the first quarter of the year, and it represented a nice jump over the $2.68 billion the company had at the end of 2024. The only thing I don't like about the deposit picture is that the company continues to have a large amount of exposure to uninsured deposits. Today, about $1.02 billion of its deposits, or 36% of the total, are uninsured. While that is down from the 40% that the company had at the end of last year, it is still above the 30% maximum that I like to see.

As deposits increased, the value of loans on the firm's books increased as well. They ended last year at $2.11 billion. But by the second quarter of this year, they had risen a bit to $2.18 billion. The largest chunk of this loan portfolio involves the $692.6 million that the company has dedicated to non-owner-occupied and multifamily properties. But honestly, this is one of the more diverse banks that I have looked at, with exposure spread across several different areas. This includes large amounts of exposure to owner-occupied commercial real estate, commercial and industrial loans, residential real estate, construction, land and raw land loans, consumer loans, and more.

Management actually provides a lot of detail regarding its loan portfolio. In the image above, you can see the exposure that it has based on certain specialized industries. There is more concentration when it comes to healthcare. But the business is also exposed to tourism, the accommodations space, the oil and gas industry, the retail market, the non-tourism aviation space, fishing, and even restaurants and breweries. This additional diversification is great to see because it lowers the overall risk profile of the business. It also helps that only 3.6% of the company's portfolio involves office assets, if we exclude another 3.3% that is a mixture of office and warehouse assets. I am generally concerned about the office market because of high and rising vacancy rates. So low exposure here is encouraging.

The value of securities in the most recent quarter amounted to $474.5 million. That was down from the $529.4 million reported at the end of last year. Over that same window of time, cash and cash equivalents went from $62.7 million up to $150 million. Debt, meanwhile, went from $33.4 million to $73.3 million.

This shows that the company is moving away from securities, boosting their cash balance, and doing so in a way that causes additional leverage. But because the overall amount of debt the business has is small, I don't really see an issue here.

The income statement has been particularly robust as of late. The second quarter of this year saw net interest income of $31.6 million. The change in the size of the company's balance sheet played a role here. But another big contributor ended up being its net interest margin, which shot up from 4.24% to 4.66%. Management has been taking advantage of high interest rates by repricing loans as they mature. In fact, as of the end of the second quarter, 32% of its loans matured or repriced over the next three months, with another 14% repricing between the three-month mark out and the 12-month mark out. 28%, meanwhile, are expected to reprice between the one-year and the three-year mark. All combined, there was 74% of the loan portfolio. This gives management the opportunity to lock down higher interest rates before they fall further.

The first half of this year saw a similar degree of growth, with net interest income of $64.3 million beating out the $53.5 million reported the previous year. A rise in the firm's net interest margin from 4.20% to 4.61% certainly helped. On top of this, the institution saw some nice growth when it came to its non-interest income. Strong growth in mortgage banking income and purchased receivable income helped to cause a surge from $9.6 million in the second quarter of 2024 to $16.6 million this year. And for the first half of this year in its entirety, the metric almost doubled from $17.4 million to $29.7 million. This was incredibly helpful in pushing overall net profits up, with those growing from $9 million in the second quarter of last year to $11.8 million this year. And for this year in its entirety so far, it has expanded from $17.2 million to $25.1 million.

The chart above shows how the company is priced relative to earnings. This assumes that the rest of this year will look like the first half of the year did on a year-over-year basis. A multiple of 8.8 is well below the 10 maximum that I like to see. And it also puts the company below any of the five firms that I compared it to in that chart. Unfortunately, I cannot say the same thing when it comes to the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples. In the chart below, you can see how the business stacks up against the same five firms using these metrics. In this case, Northrim BanCorp is actually the most expensive of the group.

This high price relative to book value and tangible book value would typically be a deal breaker for me. But I do think an exception needs to be made here because of the robust asset quality that Northrim BanCorp exhibits. In the first chart below, you can see that it has a return on assets reading of 1.48%. That is significantly above the 1% minimum that I like to see, and it puts the company leagues above the competition. The subsequent chart shows a similar level of outperformance when it comes to return on equity. I typically like to see a reading here of 10% or greater. And at 16.37%, Northrim BanCorp is a profitability juggernaut.

Credit quality also matters. And when it comes to this, the company is doing well also. At present, only 0.37% of its total assets are non-performing. I typically look for a reading here of 0.50% or lower. Furthermore, just 0.06% of its loans are between 30 and 89 days past due. This indicates that the credit quality picture of the company is robust and that near-term risk is limited.

Of course, the picture can always change as new data comes in. And at the start of this article, I mentioned that earnings for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year are just around the corner. Analysts currently believe that net revenue will come in at about $50.6 million. That would be up nicely from the $40.4 million the company reported at the same time last year. Earnings per share are also expected to grow from $0.39 to $0.59, taking net profits up from $8.8 million to $13.2 million. Even if the company comes a little short of this, I would view the picture as favorable.

Takeaway

Right now, Northrim BanCorp is doing a fine job, and I have no reason to believe that it won't moving forward. The high price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples, as well as the elevated uninsured deposit exposure, are certainly problematic in my view. But they aren't deal-breakers. In fact, when it comes to the rest of the company, things are looking great. And it's in this context, on the eve of earnings, that I believe that keeping it rated a Buy is the appropriate choice.

data.title: September Readers ID'd 17 Ideal 'Safer' Dividends In 39 Dogs
data.description:
Reader Selections

Since May, 2017, any dividend-paying stock mentioned in a message, e-mail or comment to the author has been fair game for a reader favorite listing in this series of articles. Thus, It is possible that only rogues and discontinued, or dreadful, doubtful, dividend issues may appear.

Most valuable for the writer, however, are reader comments that catch errors in calculations or changes in direction, and figures to communicate. Examples include the reader who missed my "safer" dividend follow-up articles because they contain dividend payout ratios.

However the main subject of these articles are your stock ticker comments whether they be about favorites or rogues. Be sure to mention a ticker when you comment.

The “safer” lists are now available on the regularly published side of Seeking Alpha but that has added to confusion, since my IDEAL stocks come in two flavors: (1) not so safe and (2) safe, which is preferable since the dividends are paid from cash flow, not borrowed money.

In June a reader asked if Qualified dividend stocks could be flagged. YCharts is taking that request up with their product development team. No word has come from YCharts, yet.

My ten-year collection of Dogs of The Week portfolios are reserved for members of my Seeking Alpha Dividend Dogcatcher investing group subscribers.

Still, every month readers have grumbled that they can’t find my ten Dog of the week portfolios in my Dividend Dogcatcher service on the SA Marketplace (now called Investing Groups) site.

Summary and reference guides to 10 portfolios:

This year SA has listed all my summaries of portfolio postings in my Dogcatcher Investing Group, by date:

In Published site:

August 22, 2015 for I

October 13, 2016 for II

September 12, 2017 for III

September 13, 2018 for IV (Ivy)

September 8, 2019 for V (Volio)

September 12, 2020 for VI (Vista)

October 10, 2021 for VII (Viital)

October 8, 2022 for VIII (Viking)

October 7, 2023 for IX (Ignition)

October 11, 2024 for X (Attention)

In Investing Groups site:

October 6, 2025 for XI (Elephant)

Incidentally, My Ignition (DOTW IX) portfolio launched October 6, 2023, had all its selections in place by October 4, 2024 and posted its last return on October 3, 2025.

The Attention Portfolio (DOTW X) was filled October 10 2025 and will be fully funded by October 9, 2026.

The Elephant Folio (DOTW XI) initiated October 6. 2025 will be filled with 52 member stocks in October 9, 2026, and be fully funded by October 8, 2027.

Stay tuned for the first newly formatted Toy Dog Portfolio of tiny but mighty low priced dividend dogs.

Last winter a reader asked what the XN rating in the Market Cap column of my Yield and Target Price charts means. Here is my summary of those codes:

Dogcatcher Market Cap Codes

L= $10B+

M= $2B-$10B

S= $200M-$2B

XN=<$200M

Your comments count and encourage better writing by the dogcatcher

An observation from April 2023:

“Mr. Arnold, I enjoy reading your many articles and get some great ideas from them. However I find the number of charts are a bit of overkill, many duplicating the info. I'm sure you have your set format and reasons why, but I'd be happy with only two charts; your sort by target gains and your sort by div yield. It would make it a much easier read. Thank You —pmbrandt 15Apr2023.”

I’d gladly just submit those two charts and ditch all the verbiage. However, fifteen years ago when I started submitting articles, the SA editorial team said they needed text to go with the graphics and topsy was born.

Still, there are frequent complaints of too many words for little information. In May, somebody thought I was paid by the word. No such luck.

In May. 2025 a reader, named Move on, commented on my Bloomberg Focus List article, “Death by data…just get to the point already…"

My reply: @Move on Good point! Here's a hint: Forget the text. Read the charts and graphs. Those are the dots to connect.

In January 2025 I got in trouble with readers when I neglected to define an acronym used to describe the Ben Graham Value collection. Unintroduced, I dropped a GASV acronym on them which stood for “Graham All-Star Value” stocks, dogs, or pack.

No doubt, less is more! I’ll be alert for methods to make my Dividend Kings, Dow Dogs, Aristocrats, Fortune 500 Industry Leaders, MoPays, Portfolios and all my other articles, less obfuscating.

In February, 2022 one reader suggested an option strategy for monthly-paying dividend stocks:

"You should identify where options are available on the Monthly dividend stocks. What I do is find mo-pay stocks with options, I buy and write covered calls about 6 - 12 months out. I look for a scenario where I collect the dividend and get my stock bought back at a much lower price than I pay, but pocket a premium that makes up the loss. This gives me a dividend boost, since my cost is lower. It's like a guaranteed CD with little risk."

[See a summary of ten fair-priced September ReFa/Ro Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and twenty-eight “safer” choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

More than one writer has decried my favoritism for low-priced stocks. They especially dislike my "ideal" stocks whose dividend returns from a $1k investment equal or exceed share price.

A prime example is Sirius XM Holdings Inc (SIRI); the satellite radio and pandora music catalog owner, recently spun off from Liberty media (and now listed as one of the S&P500).

A reverse-split re-priced SIRI in October 2024. Recently priced at $21.32, SIRI still easily passes my test (of dividends from $1k invested exceeding share price) with a forward dividend yield of 4.94%! A little under $1k investment now buys 46.9 shares. and they'll throw the owner a return (from that $1k invested) over 2 times the share price. Siri, however, is in the doghouse this month because of negative return for the year over 50%.

Assuming all things remain equal, SIRI dividends alone will pay back their purchase price in about 20.24 years (and that assumes the satellite radio and subscription music services can survive that long)!

In December 2023, a reader suggested my share price equal or less than the income from a $1k investment was too low. I agree with him that opportunities are lost and that many strong stocks exist at higher prices. For example, even the dividend from $10k invested in NVIDIA Corp could not buy one share of that stock. Plainly nobody buys NVDA for the dividend, just growth. (On 10/3/25 $1.00 of NVDA dividend was priced at $4,525.00)

However, I was reminded of a comment in May, 2024 that some readers take “dividends from one $1K invested” to mean the annual return from just one share. To clarify: if the single share price of a stock is $20 and the annual yield is 3.00%, the dividend paid from $1.000.00 invested is $30.00 or 1.5 times the single share price.

February 17, 2025 reader wdjaxOn responded to my suggestion that besides waiting for the market prices to drop or higher dividends to be declared for stocks, that stock splits could also make dividends from $1k invested exceed the single share price of the stock.

Stay tuned. I now include stock splits in my suggestions to those who determine the price of dividends.

Here is a useful reader report from CEFreeman.com as of May, 2025:

“Interesting! Not only have I discovered I am an accidental Income Investor, but evidently I am also a Dog Catcher. If I am understanding this correctly. I don't have $1000 to invest each time, but I strive to buy enough of something so the dividends always buy 1 share or more. Usually, I try to buy 100 shares. Sure, the positions I buy are very low priced, but I must be doing something right. I am growing my income solidly, penny by penny! Dog Catching Income Investor. I like it.

The point of this ReFa/Ro article is: “Be More Dog!” Stay tuned, Toy dog stock breeds may be coming for you!

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 30.84% To 70.61% Net Gains From Top-Ten ReFa/Ro Dogs By September, 2026.

Six of ten top ReFa/Ro Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this September, 2025 yield-based forecast for ReFa/Ro dogs, as graded by Wall St. Wizard estimates, was 60% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to September, 2026 were:

MFA Financial (MFA) was projected to net $706.14, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 8 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 70% greater than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 41.41% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten ReFa/Ro stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 20% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs."

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the summer Hamptons euphoria in the markets dies, the wall street moguls get back to work, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential pronouncements gain momentum through 2025 with the exception of very few hot tech stocks, look for most of the ReFa/Ro Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or suddenly, with an assist from trade war clouds, or a pandemic of one sort or another.
